footer.description: Strategic business development for Nordic companies and leadership teams. From insight and strategy to measurable results.

home.heroDesc: We help ambitious leadership teams navigate complexity, accelerate growth and build organisations that shape the future — not just react to it.
home.aboutP1: NorthForce Advisory was founded with a conviction: that strategic advisory should lead to real change. We combine analytical depth with operational sharpness and always work close to leadership — from diagnosis to delivery.
home.aboutP2: Our approach is rooted in the Nordic leadership tradition: transparency, trust and determination.
home.aboutQuote: Real change emerges where insight meets determination.
home.cap1Desc: We identify the critical choices and build strategies that transform entire organisations — not just individual functions.
home.cap2Desc: We lead your organisation through the digital shift with focus on business value, change management and measurable returns.
home.cap3Desc: We strengthen leaders and management teams to make better decisions, mobilise organisations and deliver results under pressure.
home.methDesc: Our process is designed to eliminate the gap between strategy and reality. Each phase has clear deliverables, ownership and metrics.
home.methQuote: Between strategy and reality, there is a method.
home.meth1Text: In-depth analysis of business, market and organisation. We map the strategic reality — not the assumptions.
home.meth2Text: Tailored strategic frameworks with clear priorities, measurable goals and a concrete action plan.
home.meth3Text: Implementation support ensuring the strategy lives in the organisation. We stay until results are delivered.
home.meth4Text: Continuous measurement, optimisation and knowledge transfer. Sustainable growth — not temporary effects.
home.ctaTitle: Every transformation begins with a conversation
home.ctaDesc: We invite you to an open conversation about your business, your challenges and your ambitions. No obligations — just an honest exchange between senior advisors and decision-makers who have seen it before.

about.label: About us
about.name: Our advisors
about.p1: NorthForce Advisory is a team of strategic advisors and business developers. With many years of experience helping companies through change and growth, we have developed a deep understanding of what it takes to navigate a complex business world.
about.p2: Our driving force is to see ambitious companies and leaders reach their full potential. The best results emerge when insight meets determination.
about.storyTitle: A journey driven by passion for results
about.storyP1: During our years in business development and leadership, we have had the privilege of working with everything from fast-growing startups to established organisations. What unites all successful transformation journeys we have been part of is one thing: the courage to act on insight.
about.storyP2: NorthForce Advisory was founded to offer what we are most passionate about — helping companies go from thought to action, and from strategy to results. Our methodology is built on combining analytical acuity with a genuine focus on people.
about.storyP3: In every engagement, we strive not only to deliver strategic recommendations, but to stand by your side throughout the implementation. For us, it is the results that count — not the presentations.
about.val1Desc: Every successful strategy begins with deep understanding. We invest time in truly understanding your business before taking a single step.
about.val2Desc: We are passionate about seeing organisations grow. Your result is our result — and we never give up until we get there.
about.val3Desc: We believe in long-term relationships. Instead of delivering a report and moving on, we work side by side with you throughout the journey.
about.val4Desc: Strategies without results are just documents. Everything we do aims at measurable change that makes a real difference.
about.ctaTitle: Let us talk about your ambitions
about.ctaDesc: We always take the time to listen. Book a call and let us explore together how we can contribute to your success.
about.credExp1: Over 15 years of experience in strategic business development in the Nordics
about.credExp2: Led transformation projects for companies from startup to publicly listed
about.credExp3: Background in management consulting, technology and leadership development
about.credExp4: Worked with management teams in Sweden, Norway, Denmark and Finland
about.personDesc: NorthForce Advisory offers over 15 years of experience in strategic business development and leadership in the Nordics.

services.label: Services
services.desc: Every business is unique. We offer a broad spectrum of services tailored to your specific needs — always focused on real, measurable value.
services.s1Desc: We help you identify growth opportunities, build sustainable strategies and execute them with clear results. Through in-depth analyses of your market, competitors and organisation, we create a strategy unique to your business.
services.s2Desc: Digital transformation is not just about technology — it is about changing how your organisation creates value. We guide you through the entire journey with focus on technology serving the business.
services.s3Desc: Many companies have strategies — few succeed in turning them into real results. Our proven methodology takes you from strategic thinking to concrete action and measurable success.
services.s4Desc: Personal coaching for business leaders and entrepreneurs who want to develop their leadership, make better decisions and navigate complex challenges with greater confidence.
services.s5Desc: A strong organisation is the foundation for sustainable growth. We help you build an organisation ready to meet the challenges of the future — with the right structure, culture and competence.
services.s6Desc: Inspiring and hands-on workshops that give your team concrete tools. Tailored to your needs and context.
services.ctaTitle: Which service is right for you?
services.ctaDesc: Not sure where to start? Book a free consultation and we will help you identify the right next step.

coaching.label: Coaching
coaching.desc: Personal coaching for those who want to grow as a leader, make better decisions and create results with greater ease.
coaching.a1Desc: Develop the ability to see the big picture, identify patterns and make decisions that create long-term value.
coaching.a2Desc: Build the confidence to make difficult decisions in complex situations — with clarity, courage and reflection.
coaching.a3Desc: Rediscover the driving force and create sustainable routines that give energy instead of draining it.
coaching.a4Desc: Clarify the vision, define the goals and create a concrete plan to reach them with precision.
coaching.fw1: CEOs and business leaders who want to take their leadership to the next level
coaching.fw4: Leaders who want to develop their ability to inspire and engage
coaching.fw5: Founders who need a sparring partner outside their own organisation
coaching.p1Desc: We start with a free call to understand your situation and your goals.
coaching.p2Desc: We design a coaching programme tailored to your specific needs.
coaching.p3Desc: We meet regularly — digitally or in person — for focused sessions.
coaching.p4Desc: Between sessions, we are available for questions and quick check-ins.
coaching.ctaDesc: The first step is always the most important. Book a free call and let us explore what coaching can do for you.

strategy.label: Methodology
strategy.desc: Our proven methodology takes you from current state to desired state — step by step, with clear goals and measurable results.
strategy.strategyQuote: Success is never a matter of chance.
strategy.p1Desc: We start by understanding your business in depth. Through interviews, data analysis and market research, we map your current situation, your strengths and the opportunities that exist.
strategy.p2Desc: With insights as the foundation, we build a strategy that is specific, measurable and achievable. We define clear goals, prioritise efforts and create an action plan with concrete milestones.
strategy.p3Desc: Now we turn strategy into action. We work closely with you and your team to ensure every initiative is executed with quality.
strategy.p4Desc: We continuously measure against set goals. Through regular analysis, we identify what works and what new opportunities arise.
strategy.p4d4: Plan for the next phase of growth
strategy.ctaTitle: Ready to go from strategy to results?
strategy.ctaDesc: Every journey begins with a first step. Book a free call and let us discuss how this methodology can be adapted for your business.

blog.label: Blog
blog.title: Insights & reflections
blog.desc: Thoughts on business development, leadership, change and growth.
blog.ctaDesc: Have thoughts about something you have read, or want to talk about how it can be applied in your business?
blog.featuredTitle: Five signs your strategy is not living in the organisation
blog.featuredExcerpt: Many companies invest months in strategic planning — but a year later, nothing has changed. The strategy document sits in a folder while the business runs on old habits. We have identified five clear warning signs and share concrete steps to break the pattern.
blog.post1Title: The quiet power of leadership: listening before you lead
blog.post1Excerpt: In our experience coaching Nordic leaders, we see a clear pattern: those who succeed best are not the ones with the answers — but those who ask the right questions. Here we share three listening techniques that transform leadership team dynamics.
blog.post2Title: Digital transformation: it is not about the technology
blog.post2Excerpt: After leading digital transformations in traditional industries, we have seen the same mistake repeated: the focus lands on systems and tools instead of the people who will use them. Here is how to break the pattern.
blog.post3Title: From uncertainty to decisiveness: a coaching journey
blog.post3Excerpt: A CEO we coached described it as standing at a crossroads every day without a compass. Six months later, he was making decisions faster and with greater confidence than ever. Here is what changed.
blog.post4Excerpt: Most companies are drowning in data but starving for insight. We share a framework we use with our clients to identify the three to five metrics that actually drive growth — and eliminate everything else.
blog.post5Title: Why most change initiatives fail — and how to avoid it
blog.post5Excerpt: 70% of all change initiatives fail. But it is rarely because of the strategy — it is because of three critical factors most leadership teams underestimate. We share what 15 years of experience has taught us.
blog.featuredBody1: ## Why strategies fail to take root
blog.featuredBody2: Most organisations invest significant time and energy into crafting a strategic plan. Workshops are held, consultants are engaged, and a carefully polished document emerges at the end. Yet far too often, the strategy never leaves the boardroom. The organisation continues operating as it always has, and the document becomes a relic — referenced occasionally, lived rarely.
blog.featuredBody3: This is not a failure of thinking. It is a failure of translation. The gap between formulating a strategy and embedding it into the daily rhythm of an organisation is where most strategic efforts quietly die.
blog.featuredBody4: ## Sign one: the strategy document lives in a folder
blog.featuredBody5: If your strategy only exists as a presentation or PDF that people need to search for, it has already lost its grip. A living strategy is visible — in conversations, in decision frameworks, in how people prioritise their week. When leaders need to remind the organisation that a strategy exists, that is a clear signal it has not been absorbed.
blog.featuredBody6: ## Sign two: operational decisions contradict strategic direction
blog.featuredBody7: Watch how decisions are made in the middle of the organisation. Are budget allocations, hiring choices, and project priorities aligned with the stated strategic direction? Or do they follow old patterns and short-term logic? When operational reality and strategic intent pull in different directions, operational reality always wins.
blog.featuredBody8: ## Sign three: middle management cannot articulate the strategy
blog.featuredBody9: Middle managers are the bridge between strategic intent and daily execution. If they cannot explain the strategy in their own words — not recite it, but genuinely translate it into what it means for their teams — then the strategy has not reached the organisation. This is often a sign that the communication has been top-down and one-directional, without space for dialogue and interpretation.
blog.featuredBody10: ## Sign four: short-term fires always win
blog.featuredBody11: Every organisation faces urgent issues. But when fire-fighting consistently takes priority over strategic work, it reveals something deeper: the organisation does not truly believe the strategy is more important than the immediate crisis. Building the discipline to protect strategic time — even when it is uncomfortable — is one of the most important leadership tasks.
blog.featuredBody12: ## Sign five: no connection between daily work and strategic goals
blog.featuredBody13: Ask any team member how their daily tasks connect to the company's strategic priorities. If they struggle to answer, the strategy has not been operationalised. People need to see themselves in the strategy — to understand how their contribution matters. Without this connection, strategy remains abstract and irrelevant to the people who ultimately determine whether it succeeds.
blog.featuredBody14: ## Moving from document to daily practice
blog.featuredBody15: Turning a strategy into a living practice requires patience, repetition, and a willingness to involve the entire organisation in the process. It means having honest conversations about what needs to change, creating feedback loops that surface resistance early, and building a rhythm of review that keeps strategic priorities visible. The best strategies are not the most brilliant — they are the ones that actually get implemented.
blog.post1Body2: In a world that celebrates decisiveness and vision, listening might seem like a passive quality. Yet in our experience working with Nordic leadership teams, it is the leaders who listen most attentively who ultimately make the best decisions. Listening is not the absence of leadership — it is one of its most powerful expressions.
blog.post1Body3: The best leaders understand that they do not have all the answers, and that the quality of their decisions depends directly on the quality of the information they receive. That information only flows freely when people feel genuinely heard.
blog.post1Body4: ## Asking questions rather than giving answers
blog.post1Body5: There is a particular kind of courage in asking questions as a leader. It requires setting aside the expectation that you should already know the answer. When a leader asks open, genuine questions — not rhetorical ones designed to steer the conversation — something shifts. The room opens up. People begin to share what they actually think rather than what they believe the leader wants to hear.
blog.post1Body6: This does not mean leaders should never share their perspective. It means the sequence matters. Listen first. Understand the landscape. Then speak with the benefit of that fuller picture.
blog.post1Body7: ## The power of silence in meetings
blog.post1Body8: Silence in a meeting can feel uncomfortable, even threatening. But it is often in those pauses that the most important thoughts emerge. When leaders resist the urge to fill every gap with words, they create space for reflection. They signal that thinking is valued, not just speaking. Some of the most productive leadership team meetings contain long stretches of quiet consideration before any conclusion is drawn.
blog.post1Body10: Listening well is not just a technique — it builds the foundation for psychological safety. When people see that their input is received without judgement, that questions are welcomed, and that dissenting views are treated as valuable rather than disloyal, they bring more of themselves to the table. This is where real innovation and honest problem-solving begin.
blog.post1Body12: The Nordic leadership model has long valued consensus and collective input. This tradition is not about avoiding conflict or slowing down decisions. At its best, it is about ensuring that when a decision is made, it carries the genuine understanding and commitment of those who must execute it. Listening is the mechanism through which this understanding is built. Leaders who master it do not just make better decisions — they build organisations that move together with clarity and conviction.
blog.post2Body2: When organisations decide to embark on a digital transformation, the first instinct is often to focus on the tools. Which platform should we adopt? What systems need replacing? How do we modernise our technology stack? These are valid questions, but they are the wrong starting point. Organisations that begin with technology frequently find themselves with expensive new systems that nobody uses effectively, or that solve yesterday's problems rather than tomorrow's opportunities.
blog.post2Body3: Digital transformation is fundamentally about how an organisation creates, delivers, and captures value. The technology is simply the enabler — not the destination.
blog.post2Body5: Every digital transformation is, at its core, a human transformation. It asks people to change how they work, how they collaborate, and sometimes how they think about their role. This is deeply personal, and it generates resistance that no amount of technology can overcome. Understanding this human dimension — the fears, the habits, the legitimate concerns about relevance and competence — is essential for any transformation that aims to last.
blog.post2Body6: Leaders who take the time to address these concerns openly, to invest in people alongside platforms, tend to see far more sustainable results.
blog.post2Body7: ## Why incremental wins beat big-bang approaches
blog.post2Body8: The allure of a complete overhaul is strong. It promises a clean break from the old and a fresh start with the new. In practice, big-bang transformations carry enormous risk. They demand that everything works at once, leave no room for learning, and often exhaust the organisation before the benefits materialise. An incremental approach — starting with visible, achievable improvements that build confidence and capability — creates momentum. Each small win demonstrates that change is possible and worthwhile, making the next step easier to take.
blog.post2Body10: Beneath every failed digital transformation is usually a culture that was not ready for it. Culture determines what behaviour is rewarded, what risks are tolerated, and how openly people share problems. A hierarchical culture that punishes failure will struggle with the experimentation that digital ways of working demand. A siloed culture will resist the cross-functional collaboration that integrated digital solutions require.
blog.post2Body11: Addressing culture does not mean launching a culture programme. It means leaders modelling the behaviour they want to see — being open about what they do not know, celebrating learning from failure, and breaking down barriers between teams. Technology follows culture, not the other way around.
blog.post3Body2: Leadership is, in many ways, a series of decisions made under imperfect conditions. Every day brings choices — some small and routine, others with consequences that ripple through the organisation for years. Over time, the sheer volume of decisions can become overwhelming. Leaders describe a growing heaviness: the fear of making the wrong call, the tendency to delay, the exhaustion of carrying responsibility for outcomes they cannot fully control.
blog.post3Body3: This is not a weakness. It is a natural consequence of leading in complex environments where the stakes are real and the information is always incomplete.
blog.post3Body4: ## Decision fatigue and its hidden cost
blog.post3Body5: Decision fatigue is well documented but rarely discussed in leadership circles. As the day wears on and the choices accumulate, the quality of decisions deteriorates. Leaders begin to default to the safe option, the familiar path, or simply to postpone. The cost of this is rarely visible in any single moment, but over time it creates stagnation. Opportunities pass. Problems grow. The organisation senses hesitation and begins to lose confidence in the direction.
blog.post3Body6: Recognising decision fatigue is the first step toward addressing it. Simple structural changes — protecting time for important decisions, reducing trivial choices, and building decision-making frameworks — can make a significant difference.
blog.post3Body7: ## The value of a sparring partner
blog.post3Body8: One of the loneliest aspects of leadership is having few people to think out loud with. Board members have their own agendas. Direct reports may tell you what they think you want to hear. Friends outside the business may lack context. A skilled sparring partner — someone who understands business complexity, asks challenging questions, and has no stake in the outcome — can be transformative. Not because they provide answers, but because they help leaders clarify their own thinking.
blog.post3Body10: Coaching at its best is a process of building self-awareness. It helps leaders recognise their patterns: the situations that trigger avoidance, the biases that shape their perception, the strengths they underuse and the habits that no longer serve them. This awareness does not arrive in a single moment of insight. It develops gradually, through reflection, honest feedback, and the willingness to sit with uncomfortable truths about oneself.
blog.post3Body11: ## The difference between knowing and doing
blog.post3Body12: Most leaders already know what they should do. The challenge is rarely a lack of knowledge. It is the gap between understanding the right course of action and actually taking it — especially when it involves difficult conversations, unpopular decisions, or personal change. Coaching bridges this gap not by adding more knowledge, but by building the inner clarity and confidence needed to act. The journey from uncertainty to decisiveness is not about becoming someone different. It is about becoming more fully yourself as a leader.
blog.post4Body2: Modern organisations have access to more data than at any point in history. Dashboards glow with metrics. Reports pile up. Yet many leadership teams confess privately that they feel less informed than ever. The sheer volume of available data creates noise that drowns out signal. When everything is measured, nothing stands out. When every metric is treated as important, none of them drive action.
blog.post4Body3: This is the measurement paradox: the more we measure, the less we may actually understand about what is driving — or undermining — our performance.
blog.post4Body5: Not all metrics are created equal. Vanity metrics look impressive in presentations but have little connection to the outcomes that truly matter. They might show activity without impact, volume without quality, or growth without profitability. Meaningful indicators, by contrast, reveal the underlying health and trajectory of the business. They are often harder to measure, slower to move, and less flattering — but they tell the truth about where value is being created and where it is being eroded.
blog.post4Body6: The discipline lies in distinguishing between the two, and having the honesty to stop tracking metrics that feel good but fail to inform decisions.
blog.post4Body7: ## A framework for identifying what truly matters
blog.post4Body8: Start with the outcome you are trying to achieve and work backwards. What are the few critical drivers that most directly influence that outcome? For each driver, what is the earliest and most reliable indicator of whether you are on track? This approach typically yields a small number of metrics — often fewer than you expect. The power of a focused measurement framework is not just in what it includes, but in what it deliberately excludes. Every metric you remove reduces noise and sharpens attention.
blog.post4Body10: A metric earns its place when it consistently informs a decision. If a number rises or falls and nobody changes their behaviour as a result, that metric is not serving its purpose. Before adding any new measurement, ask: what decision will this help us make? If the answer is unclear, the metric is likely adding complexity without value.
blog.post4Body12: Removing metrics takes courage. People become attached to the numbers they have always tracked. Teams build routines around reports that may have outlived their usefulness. Letting go of measurement feels like losing control. But the opposite is often true. By narrowing focus to the vital few indicators, leadership teams gain clarity, speed, and the ability to act with conviction. Measuring what truly matters is not about having less data — it is about having better judgement about which data deserves attention.
blog.post5Body2: Most organisations recognise the need for change. Strategies are formulated, initiatives are launched, and communication plans are drafted. Yet time and again, these efforts stall, lose momentum, or quietly fade away. The uncomfortable truth is that change failure is rarely caused by a flawed strategy or the wrong tools. It is caused by underestimating the human and organisational dynamics that determine whether change takes hold or falls apart.
blog.post5Body3: Understanding why change fails is the essential first step toward making it succeed.
blog.post5Body4: ## The first critical factor: leadership commitment
blog.post5Body5: Change requires visible, sustained commitment from the top. Not a one-time announcement or a keynote presentation, but ongoing engagement with the process — showing up, asking questions, removing obstacles, and demonstrating through behaviour that this change matters. When leaders delegate change entirely to a project team and return to business as usual, the organisation reads the signal clearly: this is not truly a priority. Leadership commitment must be felt, not just communicated.
blog.post5Body6: ## The second critical factor: middle management engagement
blog.post5Body7: Middle managers are the most underestimated force in any change initiative. They translate strategy into daily operations. They set the tone for their teams. They decide, often unconsciously, how much energy and attention the change receives at the level where work actually happens. When middle managers are not genuinely engaged — when they feel bypassed, uninformed, or sceptical — they become a silent barrier. Investing in their understanding and involvement is not optional. It is essential.
blog.post5Body8: ## The third critical factor: employee participation
blog.post5Body9: People support what they help create. When change is designed and imposed from above, it meets resistance — sometimes open, more often passive. Involving employees early, listening to their concerns, and incorporating their insights does not slow the process down. It builds the foundation of ownership that sustains change long after the initial enthusiasm fades. Participation is not about asking permission. It is about tapping into the knowledge and commitment that exist at every level of the organisation.
blog.post5Body11: Most change communication suffers from two problems: it is too infrequent, and it is too focused on the what rather than the why. People can absorb new information about processes and structures, but they will only truly engage when they understand the reason behind the change — and when they can see how it connects to something they care about. Effective change communication is not a campaign. It is an ongoing conversation that evolves as the change progresses, honestly addressing setbacks as well as successes.
blog.post5Body13: The most resilient change initiatives are anchored in a clear and compelling reason for change. Not a business case full of projections, but a genuine answer to the question: why must we change, and what is at stake if we do not? When this why is understood and shared across the organisation, it provides an anchor that holds steady when the process gets difficult — as it inevitably will. Starting with why is not a communication technique. It is the foundation upon which lasting change is built.

cases.label: Cases
cases.desc: Every engagement is unique, but the goal is always the same — real, measurable results that make a difference.
cases.c1Title: Growth strategy for a service company in transition
cases.c1Challenge: A mid-sized B2B services company had experienced steady growth for several years, but noticed it had suddenly plateaued. New competitors with digital business models were capturing market share, while existing clients were beginning to demand different types of solutions. The leadership team disagreed on the way forward — some wanted to invest in new services, others wanted to scale existing ones. The company needed a clear strategic direction the entire organisation could rally behind.
cases.c1Approach: We began with a thorough diagnosis: in-depth interviews with the leadership team, key clients and employees, combined with market and competitive analysis. The analysis revealed that the company's core competence was stronger than they believed — but it was aimed at shrinking segments. We helped them reposition their offering, identify three new customer segments with high growth potential and build a long-term strategic plan with clear milestones.
cases.c1r1: New strategic direction decided and anchored across the organisation
cases.c1r2: Three new growth areas identified — the first generating revenue within the first half-year
cases.c1r3: Significant revenue increase already within the first year
cases.c1r4: Strengthened position as leading player in their segment
cases.c2Title: Digital maturity in a traditional industry
cases.c2Challenge: An established industry player with a long history and strong market position realised their ways of working were no longer sustainable. Customers expected digital experiences the company could not deliver. Internally, processes were manual and fragmented, leading to inefficiency and frustration. Several previous digitalisation projects had been started but never completed — there was widespread scepticism in the organisation towards yet another change initiative.
cases.c2Approach: We conducted a digital maturity assessment mapping where the organisation stood and what could realistically be achieved. Instead of a grand transformation plan, we chose an incremental approach: quick, visible wins first to build trust, followed by deeper systemic changes. We worked closely with both management and employees to ensure buy-in at every step.
cases.c2r1: Digital transformation plan anchored with both leadership and employees
cases.c2r2: Customer experience digitised with measurably increased satisfaction
cases.c2r3: Internal processes streamlined with significant time savings in core processes
cases.c2r4: Organisation with new digital competence and genuine willingness to change
cases.c3Title: Leadership coaching during a critical transition
cases.c3Challenge: A newly appointed CEO of a mid-sized company faced a challenging situation. The predecessor had left after a turbulent period, internal trust was low and several key employees were considering leaving. At the same time, the company needed to carry out a necessary reorganisation and find a new direction. The CEO had strong expertise but felt uncertain in the new role — particularly around leading change and managing conflicting expectations from the board, leadership team and employees.
cases.c3Approach: We designed an individual coaching programme with regular sessions over an extended period. The focus was on three areas: building a clear leadership vision, developing the ability to communicate change, and managing the personal pressure that comes with a CEO role. In parallel, we served as a strategic sparring partner for the business decisions that needed to be made.
cases.c3r1: Clear leadership vision formulated and communicated to the entire organisation
cases.c3r2: Reorganisation completed while retaining key personnel
cases.c3r3: Significantly increased trust from the board and leadership team measured through internal evaluation
cases.c3r4: Personal development the CEO described as decisive for succeeding in the role
cases.ctaDesc: Every success story begins with a conversation.

privacy.label: Legal
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.desc: Your privacy is important to us. Here we describe how we collect, use and protect your personal data.
privacy.s1Content: NorthForce Advisory based in Sweden is the data controller for the processing of your personal data in connection with the use of this website and our services.
privacy.s2Content: We collect personal data that you voluntarily provide to us through our contact and booking forms. This data may include name, email address, phone number, company name as well as messages and other information you provide in forms.
privacy.s3Content: We use your personal data to respond to your enquiries, book calls with you, provide our services and communicate with you regarding ongoing or planned engagements. We never share your personal data with third parties without your consent, unless required by law.
privacy.s4Content: The processing of your personal data is based on your consent and our legitimate interest in responding to enquiries and providing services you have requested.
privacy.s5Content: We store your personal data for as long as necessary to fulfil the purpose for which it was collected. We take appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect your data against unauthorised access, loss or manipulation.
privacy.s6Content: Under the GDPR, you have the right to request access to, rectification and erasure of your personal data. You also have the right to object to processing, request restriction of processing and the right to data portability. You may withdraw your consent at any time.
privacy.s7Content: This website may use cookies to improve your experience. Cookies are small text files stored on your device. You can change your cookie settings in your browser at any time.
privacy.s8Content: We reserve the right to update this privacy policy. For material changes, we will inform you on our website. Last updated: January 2025.
privacy.s9Content: Have questions about our handling of personal data? Contact us at kontakt@northforceadvisory.se. You also have the right to file a complaint with the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY).
